speaker: interviewer
text: May 3, 1996-- tell us about the orphanage in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we're-- we headed off. It was about 19, 20 kids-- different ages, different problems. And they're stealing from each other. And-- and I had one-- a dear friend of mine, who survived and lives in  Paris now. He was like my assistant, helping me. He was also very good with children. And we-- Our role was to deal with them and get them ready for-- for departure for Israel. Because, at that time, there was only illegal immigration to Israel. And they didn't have any papers to begin with, so we had to make sure that we wait for the right moment, and when they can smuggle them out. So-- So I-- We had a little office there. And I did not want-- I didn't have an apartment, but I didn't want to sleep in the office. And I wanted to sleep with them day and night because they could rape each other. I mean, it was really unbelievable. And we had only-- this orphanage was a room and a half. And we did all the cooking and everything there. And it took a lot of pain and talking and yelling and dealing with them. And it was wonderful to see how they were changing slowly. Most of them fell in love with this beautiful blonde girl, who was absolutely gorgeous and beautiful, noble character. As a matter of fact, later on in-- she left for Israel, and-- and she got married, happily married, and had two children. And we got them all out. So that was really--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get them out? Who helped get them out?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was like a Bricha organization that was smuggling them out to Italy. And for that, you had to pay a lot of money to smuggle kids out. And you-- you couldn't have 12 or 15. You had to do them slowly with three kids, six kids. And certain girls who worked with them-- I know a friend of mine who was involved in that. She would have to take them by train to certain places. And you had to also make sure that they don't attract attention because there were too many, and they would start suspecting. It-- it was an illegal problem, and very, very difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year are we talking about now?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're talking about '45. So that was still very difficult at that time. And then, finally, I found myself one room on-- in the old part of the city, of course. No [? kidding. ?] But to have a room at that time in Warsaw where everything was destroyed was a big thing. And-- but still, it was within a few blocks that I could be in. Anytime there was a problem, I could be in the orphanage right away. And-- And I continued with my education, with my medical school. And one day, I am working in this orphanage home, and trying to-- this was in May-- trying to plan things for them. And a man, very pleasant-looking man, comes in, is looking for work. That's my future husband. And I was outside. I remember waiting for someone. And he's asking me if there's anything he can do. And I interview him. And then I said, you sound intelligent enough. If you want to be my assistant, I can-- I can employ you. And, of course, it wasn't a question of money because nobody was getting paid. And so that I employed him. And that's how I got to know him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did employment mean? That you had--

speaker: interviewee
text: Employment means that, let's say, if a package comes from the United States, I will be in charge of-- if I needed the jacket, I knew that I can get the jacket. That's a priority. That they send money for the organization. So there was enough, let's say, for the children. And I could also eat with them. So I could cover my necessities. But let's say they wouldn't pay for rent. And how I paid for rent, I really don't know. Maybe the organization must have paid for-- for the rent.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you paying for school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was-- and the Russians would pay you, give you a stipend, like $70 or 70 rubles, all the books. That-- that was always the case with the Russians that they would always pay for your schooling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even in Warsaw now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. And I never-- never needed any money. Anytime I went to a school, the Russians would take-- nobody pays in Russia for-- for education and for the-- for textbooks. The thing is, though, not too many textbooks. And, of course, to them, it is an advantage because it's criminal what they are doing. But I don't know if they do it now. But when I went to school, you had to take, let's say, a course-- even in medical school-- about Stalin and Lenin, about their philosophy. This was more important than anything else. And don't forget, those were times that it wasn't really-- everything very well-organized-- was very chaotic.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I don't think even professors were paid. And so, it was-- it was, the war was still in the air at that time. Things were not normal. So in Warsaw, I met a very nice man. He would come, very often, to the organization.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a handsome, handsome pilot, in a  Polish uniform. He was the personal pilot to the president of Poland, Bierut. And he was also the head of the-- of the military airport. And that was a big thing because he had all the wine. And the-- the head, and as a pilot to the president, had all the wine and liquor, and best beef, and fish and sardines-- everything under the sun. And he started to talk, and happens so that his sister was dating my former husband. They were all from Warsaw. His sister, the pilot, and [? Feliks, ?] used to date each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, [? Feliks ?] is the man whom you hired to be your assistant at the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, my future husband. So-- so of course, they became very good friends. And that pilot didn't know [? Feliks ?] at all before, so they became very good friends. So-- but the pilot liked me, and he met me before I met [? Feliks. ?] And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and-- he used to come very often. And I was busy with school and with work. And when [? Feliks ?] came, I started to date, eventually. In the beginning, I didn't think about it. He would come quite often, but I was so busy, I didn't think about it. But then, I saw that pilot liked me too. And he started to talk about it. I said, look, you know, I'm-- I'm right now going out with [? Feliks. ?] And I have a cousin in Krakow. And she's more or less my age.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if you like me, you'll like her too. Why don't I get in touch with her and invite her to Warsaw and see if you like her? And he said, OK. And I called Krakow. And her brother gave her the money.

speaker: interviewee
text: She came here. I told her, you-- I have a date for you. And she was already involved with a lawyer there, but anyway, she came. In three weeks, they got married. And it was really wonderful because I married them there. And then we went to another town. And just-- we meet with six or seven people. And she got married to him. And she's in Israel with him and has three children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was wonderful because three weeks, she just went back. And her brother was very upset because the brother liked the lawyer she was dating, and she wasn't crazy about him. So I said, so he calls me, what are you doing? I said, I'm telling you. He's a very nice guy. You will like him. Anyway-- Was he Jewish, the--

speaker: interviewee
text: --yeah yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How does a Jewish, how does the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Unusual. That was absolutely unusual. He really looked like a-- like a handsome Polish officer. I mean, if you looked at him-- if you think of Prince Philip, he's like this kind of -- he really looks a little bit like him too. When I walked with him along the streets in Warsaw, every woman looked back. He, even now, is still very, good looking, very, close-mouthed, [? very ?] [? busy, ?] you know, and very nice young-- and was a very nice, young man and very interesting. So it worked out very well. So then we stayed in Warsaw for a while. And then we talked about-- oh, my family found out--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your family found out--

speaker: interviewee
text: --that I was alive, through Joint-- they were looking. And by the way, before I finish, so when [? Feliks ?] came, I knew that he came from a concentration camp, and looked very, very bad. And as far as I'm concerned, didn't have anybody. At this time-- time, I didn't know, I found out about it in Israel last-- when I was there last year. For the first time, I spoke with this pilot, with [? Mertick ?]. That pilot was walking along the streets in Warsaw, and a woman approached him. She recognized him from the picture that his sister showed her in Austria. She approached him and asked him if he is Mr. [? Lux ?]. And he says, yes. And she told me who he is-- who she is, and she came to Warsaw because his sister asked her to find her father, who survived. And she's also looking for her brother. And hears what his brother's name, and it happens to be [? Feliks ?] is her brother. And if you would read it, you wouldn't believe it. So he says, I see him almost every day. So she says-- and then he told her that his father is in the hospital, sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if she wants, she can go to the hospital. And he would bring [? Feliks ?] to the hospital. And he comes in-- [? Feliks ?] comes with him to the hospital, and opens up the door, and here was the sister. That he-- When they parted from the Warsaw ghetto, they all made a agreement that whoever comes first to Warsaw, whoever survived, and they found the place where they would put their name.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when he came to Warsaw, there was no name on the stone. Now, when she came to Warsaw, she already saw his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she knew that he is alive. So, of course, it was quite a reunion. And-- and [? Feliks ?] never told me about it. So I didn't know that he-- [? Feliks ?] had a sister, and also a mother, because the mother and sister survived in camp together. In Austria, they-- they survived the camp together. So-- And I have no idea why Feliks didn't tell me that his mother and sister-- maybe he told it, I-- I would be afraid he will not go on to Israel because I said I'm going to Israel, and this is it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know why. I have no idea. But I found that this last time, when I was in Israel. And-- and [? Mertick ?], the pilot, assumed that I know that-- that [? Feliks ?] saw his sister in Warsaw. So we decided to go to France. He lived in France before the war. He studied in Paris in L'Ecole des Beaux-Arts. This is where he had his training. And then my family found out that I was alive. They started to stall me with letters and cables and the telephone-- I should come to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, which family is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the three brothers that my father had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your uncles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uncle Joe was the one who-- he was comfortable financially, so better were than the others. So he was the one who took care of it. But all three of them--

speaker: interviewer
text: And he found you through the Red Cross, the Joint--

speaker: interviewee
text: --through the Joint, through the American organizations he found me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, meanwhile, had your sister found you? Had you found your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Meanwhile, yeah, so I went to, I found out that my sister survived. And from Paris, I-- I found out in Warsaw that my sister survived. So [? Feliks ?] promised that he will go with me to Germany to-- to find-- to find her. And so we went to Paris. And, wait, one minute. First, we went to-- went to Paris. Yes, and we stayed there. And we stayed there for a while. And we lived there for a while. And I was in touch with her-- I was not in touch with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Feliks, ?] right away, started to work as a painter. And I just waited to-- to see how I can go on to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you live on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [? Feliks ?] was working already.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a painter?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a painter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And selling things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Selling things. He did-- As a matter of fact, he was selling a lot of paintings in hiding, when he was in hiding. I have to tell you about that, maybe. Because he was known as an accomplished painter. So, and when he was hiding, he-- he supported a lot of people in the-- in hiding and was making money on the paintings. And he was also doing commercial art and making money from that. And I think--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get to Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Paris? I got-- We got-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] in 1945, from-- in September '44, I was in medical school, in the war in '45, in Warsaw, and at the end of '45, we went for France. We left for France. Now, so I-- I found out that my sister, also survived, so-- and that she lived in Frankfurt. So I-- we went to Frankfurt. And-- But she didn't know anything about my sister. So, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she know what had happened to her family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister-in-law?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, to her baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only about my brother, but not about the baby. She probably doesn't know. And I-- we never even touched it. For years, she was keeping his picture on the-- on the table. But now I see that she doesn't have it. And so I found that there was a camp that keeps Poles in-- in Germany. And that some Jews are there too. Anyway, so I went. And [? Feliks ?] said, then, let's go. You see, we went there. And-- But we didn't know if she is there or not. And I come out of the-- of the train, and I see two people. And somebody is pointing at me. And-- and I look again, looking there, and I see is someone saying, well, [? Janet ?] is here. And then, all of a sudden, I said to him, I said, this old woman is my-- my sister. She look like an old, old woman, a young girl. She lived in terrible conditions. And I was already stylish, and I was already from Paris. And-- And I said to myself, oh, my God. And she was-- her whole face was like black. And what happened-- she got up in the morning. And she lived in one room on the camp. And she was teaching Catholic children religion and-- and not admitting that she was Jewish. And she lived in one room without water, without soap, without food. And they took in a Polish woman to-- to-- because she had already a baby, and I didn't know. It was a boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister had a baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: A baby. She was already married, and I didn't know. And she got up in the morning, and she says to her husband, you know, [? Janet ?] is coming this morning. Let's go to the station. He says, what are you talking about?

speaker: interviewer
text: She says, I'm telling you. [? Janet's ?] coming this morning. He says, out of the blue? And they argue. She says, again, it was a walk like a 10-, seven-mile walk, like [? 10 ?] kilometer. So this woman, a very nice Polish woman she have for the baby. So she says to her-- to him, why do you argue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Supposedly, she's wrong. She wants to do it. It will make if you go with her. So he finally gave in. And he recognized me from a picture. And he looked, and he was the one who says, here is [? Janet. ?] Because he saw pictures my sister had because I never knew him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally, when I brought her-- it took god knows how long before she really came to herself. And so listen to this. So she was hoping that someday I will come, and she remembered that I had-- I liked pickled herring. So she would not eat-- a-- [INAUDIBLE] a piece of pickled herring that when I come, I'll have it. So when I came-- and this was my treatment-- came, and the room was so dark.

speaker: interviewee
text: The linen-- the sheets are so black. It was-- it was like a cave. So I don't have to tell you what was the reunion. And so we came down, and we stayed with them. And first thing was I wanted to stay at the Jewish camp so she refused. And I said, the conditions are better in a Jewish camp because the United States were helping.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was her husband Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Helping, yes, helping the camps with food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was her husband Jewish, your sister's husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But he was Russian. So he was taken for work for-- in Russia. So-- And he lost his entire family there. Anyway, so she didn't want to go. She said she's comfortable here. She was still afraid to say, admit, that she was Jewish, I imagine. She went through an awful lot. And I left her with days that I would take her with me, that I would bring her over. So when my uncle found out that I was survived and got in touch with me, I told him that my sister survived too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was very anxious to bring us both. And says, first, why don't they come? So, of course, I don't want to come because I already have [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] an attachment to [? Feliks. ?] We were already together. So he said, look, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- so I left for France.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here, again, at that time, a lot of Americans would come on business to-- to Paris. So anytime there was a-- some kind of associative of his-- he was in business. He was very comfortable. He would send me a few dollars too. And that maybe then will answer the question of how I was doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were super nice-- the three brothers. And-- and this friend would always try to convince me to the States that they want me to come. And they finally said, why don't you come, just for a visit? And like I said, all right, finally. And Feliks said, if they want you so badly, why don't you go? I will stay, and you'll come back. So I went there, and I fell in love with the United States as soon I-- I saw the airport-- immediately loved. Most of my friends didn't feel that way. And my entire family-- children and everybody under the sun-- was there greeting me, and made me feel very, very comfortable.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they used to fight over, where to-- the first week I went to Queens College to learn some English. I started to write letters to [? Feliks ?] that I think you have a future here. And you'll be happier because he loved Paris. I did too, but he really spent many years there. And my-- my uncle in the beginning wasn't very keen because he said, ah, it's a big deal. You should marry an American here. It doesn't have to be someone. But he saw that I'm really serious about it. So he finally said, all right, I'll bring-- they paid for my trip, of course. And they paid the lawyer because they wanted to accelerate it. And so then the telephone started to go back and forth, and [? Feliks, ?] in the beginning, was very apprehensive. Finally, I said, you will come, and also for a month. So we extended my visit visa for another two months. And he came, and we got married in my uncle's house. And-- and that's also then we stayed in New York. And Feliks, right away, went to work, and had a very good position, and I worked. And then we had to leave for Montreal, for Canada, because if you come for a visit, you cannot stay. You have to leave the country and ask for a re-entry, apply again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it took us two years. And we finally-- also, my uncle worked very hard on it because it was a very, very difficult at that time. And I was not a displaced person. I had the Polish passport. And that was much more difficult. The Polish quota was more difficult to get in, under Polish quota. So we came back. And, again, both of us went to work and saved a little bit of money. And then I told you I had a friend who helped us with a loan to-- in the bank. And we felt if we buy a farm, [? Feliks ?] will be able to work better. And I'll work for a while. And that's what we did. What kind of work did [? Feliks ?] do when you first came here?

speaker: interviewee
text: He, first of all, he painted, and then he did commercial-- commercial art.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I started to work for a firm. And they liked my legs, so they-- I started to model and become a food-- food model. And they are very, very nice to me. Gave me, every week, a raise. I started with $5. And I was-- so the farm was near Princeton, so I used to commute to the city by train. And then [? Feliks ?] would take me and pick me up. And one day I came. And I found him dead. So, as a matter of fact, we were getting ready for a party. And [INAUDIBLE] lived in the same area. And we tried to become friends with all the artists-- it was artist colony. And so it was Friday night, and instead of the party, I find him. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to Feliks?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to Feliks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Heart attack. And so then I-- so going back to my sister. So when I came to Canada, I started to-- to again, see if how first I can bring her. Oh, first, I brought her to Paris. And, also, I was lucky because-- thanks to my brother and his connections from before the war-- through some Jewish guy that he knew, they helped me to-- with an apartment that was impossible to get at that time in Paris, on the suburbs, an apartment, because you couldn't simply get anything. And so he was expecting-- one Jewish guy-- was expecting his family to come, also, survivors to stay in this place that he had for them. But he allowed us to stay until they came. So I had a chance to-- to bring-- that was a good occasion-- to bring my sister and her family, her husband, and the most beautiful child. Then I went-- when I went to Canada, I brought her to Canada. Meanwhile, in Paris, [? Feliks ?] got him a job. He learned to design gloves. So they-- so he made a living. And a little bit, we helped them. And then when they came to Canada, we got them already a job there. He learned, again, a different kind model-- how to-- how to do gloves. And two friends, and when I came to the United States, I brought them to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- you had been so determined to go to Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Palestine, yeah. You see that so I knew that [? Feliks ?] will never do that. He just thought that he might, but he realize that he wanted to stay in Paris and didn't want to go. And then, when we came to United States-- it's a terrible thing to say-- but I really fell in love with this country. And I didn't want to give it up. I-- I just, from the minute I came here-- and you know I have so many friends. Some of them went back, and some of them hated it for years. And I loved every moment of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you fall in love with this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I tell you, first of all, I felt very free. I even write it in my book that I-- I wrote. And I have put it aside, that, somehow, I took my sister on her arm, we walked along the streets. And, of course, we went through so much. And I, for the first time in my life, because in France, also, you have identity to carry with you. And they can stop you and ask you for your papers. But for the first time, in a country, they don't care who you are. You walk, you talk, you say anything you want. I felt very good. And my uncles were-- gave me a very nice home. They were simply fighting over me. I had to stay in each house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- they were warm to me. And people were very nice to me. And-- And no matter what kind of job I got, every week, they gave me a raise. And I really was good. As a matter of fact, when I-- when I started to work, and I-- I told, after [? Feliks ?] died--
